elements.sent-2411-001: We examine the vacuum state and its corresponding renormalized stress-energy tensor (RSET) in static horizonless regular spacetime in both two- and four-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using the local field formulation of the anomaly-induced effective action, we show that the regularities of the spacetime and the RSET dictate the appropriate vacuum state.
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, through a case study under the horizonless Bardeen-type spacetime, we demonstrate that the preferred vacuum state is not the Boulware vacuum, but a non-trivial one with a different RSET profile.
elements.sent-2411-004: In curved spacetime, quantum fields exhibit an interesting behavior known as the trace (or conformal) anomaly \cite{Capper:1974ic, Capper:1975ig, Deser:1976yx, Duff:1977ay (see also \cite{Birrell:1982ix, Parker:2009uva, Hu:2020luk, Duff_1994 for comprehensive review).}}
elements.sent-2411-005: A curved geometry generally induces gravitational vacuum polarization \cite{Hawking:1974rv, Davies:1974th, Hawking:1975vcx, Wald:1975kc, Parker:1975jm and/or dynamical particle production \cite{Parker:1968mv, Parker:1969au, Sexl:1969ix, Zeldovich:1970si .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Such effects modify the expectation value of the quantum field's stress-energy tensor (SET) operator, which is described as the regularization and renormalization procedures \cite{Wald:1978ce, 1977RSPSA.354...59D, Fulling:1977jm .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Consequently, the renormalized stress-energy tensor (RSET) and its trace have a non-trivial expectation value.
elements.sent-2411-008: Even a conformally coupled massless field, whose SET is traceless at the classical level, acquires a non-zero anomalous trace value.
elements.sent-2411-009: This trace anomaly of RSET is first discussed in \cite{Capper:1974ic, Capper:1975ig, Deser:1976yx, Duff:1977ay , and then applied to the case of a massless field in the black hole (BH) spacetime \cite{Christensen:1977jc, Candelas:1980zt, Frolov:1987gw, Anderson:1994hg to study Hawking radiation \cite{Hawking:1974rv, Hawking:1975vcx .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In the meantime, the cosmological spacetime case has been discussed \cite{Bunch:1977sc, Davies:1977ze, Page:1982fm, Brown:1986jy , and extended to more general spacetimes \cite{Parker:1978gh , as well as the investigation of the backreaction on the spacetime (semiclassical gravity) \cite{1974JETP...39..742L, Grishchuk:1977zz, Guth:1980zm, HAJICEK19809, Bardeen:1981zz .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The value of trace anomaly is determined by the local geometry, which is also responsible for regularizing the UV divergence of SET.
elements.sent-2411-012: On the other hand, the value of each component of RSET significantly depends also on the quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-013: The vacuum state of the quantum field in curved spacetime must be specified on some Cauchy surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-014: The choice of vacuum is closely related to the boundary conditions at the spacetime boundaries, as we will demonstrate explicitly later.
elements.sent-2411-015: Therefore, the choice of vacuum exhibits global properties of the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the case of the four-dimensional Schwarzschild spacetime, there are three well-known vacuum choices: \begin{inparaenum [(I)] \item The Israel-Hartle-Hawking vacuum \cite{Israel:1976ur, Hartle:1976tp , which describes a BH in thermal equilibrium, \item The Unruh vacuum \cite{Unruh:1976db , which corresponds to an evaporating or collapsing BH with non-zero flux, \item The Boulware vacuum \cite{Boulware:1974dm, Boulware:1975fe , which coincides with the Minkowski vacuum as $r \rightarrow \infty$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, it is well known that this vacuum leads to a divergent RSET near the horizon.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this study, we extend the analysis to the case of horizonless regular spacetimes with spherical symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-019: One key difference from BH cases is the regularity of the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-020: This requires the quantum fields and RSET to be regular everywhere in the bulk of the spacetime, and imposes different boundary conditions leading to the desired vacuum condition.
elements.sent-2411-021: Another difference arises from the global structure of the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-022: Spacetimes with black hole horizons are not isomorphic to those without horizons, leading to different possible vacuum choices.
elements.sent-2411-023: As a result, based on the local field formulation for the anomaly-induced effective action explained later, we demonstrate that the Boulware vacuum is not consistent with the central regularities in four-dimensional horizonless spacetime through a case study of a horizonless Bardeen-type spacetime \cite{1968qtr..conf...87B, Carballo-Rubio:2022nuj , while it is consistent in the two-dimensional case.}
elements.sent-2411-024: There are some previous discussions in \cite{Carballo-Rubio:2017tlh, Arrechea:2021xkp, Reyes:2023fde, Arrechea:2023oax which focused on the effect of massless fields on the geometry of horizonless compact stars such as a uniform density star.}
elements.sent-2411-025: One of the main findings was that the semiclassical effect leads to exceeding the Buchdahl limit, which is the maximum allowable compactness of a star.
elements.sent-2411-026: All of the aforementioned works assumed the Boulware vacuum, so that extra regularization (e.g. introducing a cutoff length) around the center can be necessary to address the central divergence.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our work sheds light on the validity of this assumption, illustrating the relationship between the central regularity of RSET and the choice of vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-028: To systematically investigate the relation among the spacetime structure, vacuum state, and the RSET, we adopted the local auxiliary field (``conformalon'') formulation \cite{Riegert:1984kt, Shapiro:1994ww, Balbinot:1999ri, Balbinot:1999vg, Mazur:2001aa, Mottola:2006ew, Barcelo:2011bb, Shen:2015zya of the trace anomaly.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Typically, each regularization method heavily relies on the background spacetime, making it challenging to extend the investigation to arbitrary spacetime scenarios, including those without horizons.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, by using the local field formulation, the analysis of quantum effects can be simplified to solving the profiles of local fields with boundary conditions that correspond to different vacuum choices.
elements.sent-2411-031: Although the application for non-conformally flat spacetime cases gives not exact but just approximation results, this formulation makes it much easier to extend the study to arbitrary geometries and provides a clearer understanding of the possible vacuum states.
elements.sent-2411-032: In this paper, we organize the content as follows: First, we start with a discussion of the 2-D case in \secref{sec:2-D , providing a brief introduction to the local auxiliary field formulation in \secref{sec:2-D_general .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Subsequently, after a review of the two-dimensional Minkowski and Schwarzschild spacetime cases, we investigate the possible vacuum and the resulting RSET in a horizonless static spacetime in \secref{sec:2-D_sss .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Then we move on to the four-dimensional case in \ref{sec:4-D .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Afterward, we analyze the case of a horizonless static spacetime with spherical symmetry through a case study in a Bardeen-type scenario in \secref{sec:4-D_horizonless .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, \secref{sec:discussion is dedicated to discussion and conclusion.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The appendix explains the junction condition of the local fields concerning their application to general compact star geometries.
elements.sent-2411-038: As conventions, the speed of light $c$ and the Newton constant $G_N$ are taken as unity.
elements.sent-2411-039: We use $\qty{-1,1,1, \cdots, 1}$ as the metric signs, and other curvature conventions are based on \cite{Carroll:2004st .}

elements.sent-2411-040: Based on \cite{Mottola:2006ew , we begin by reviewing the trace anomaly and corresponding local action in two-dimensional spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-041: The trace anomaly in two-dimensional spacetime is expressed as \cite{Birrell:1982ix \begin{equation}\ev{ T^a_a }=\frac{N}{24 \pi} R. \label{eq:Anom2d}\end{equation} The corresponding anomalous effective action that reproduces this trace anomaly can be written as \cite{Riegert:1984kt, Mazur:2001aa \begin{equation}S_{anom}^{(2)}[g] =\frac{Q^2}{16 \pi} \int \dd[2]{x} \sqrt{-g} \int \dd[2]{x^{\prime}} \sqrt{-g^{\prime}} R(x) \square^{-1}\qty(x, x^{\prime}) R(x^{\prime}) , \label{eq:NLaction2d}\end{equation} where $Q^2 = - N /6$ for the case with $N$ massless matter fields.\footnote{In the case with the metric perturbations besides matter fields, the number $N$ should be replaced with $N-25$ \cite{Knizhnik:1988ak, David:1988hj, Distler:1988jt .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The term $\square^{-1}\qty(x, x^{\prime )$ denotes Green's function corresponding to the inverse of the scalar d'Alembertian operator, which indicates the non-local nature of the RSET in addition to the dependence on the local curvature of the spacetime.}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Proper vacuum state and RSET in static horizonless spacetime with spherical symmetry \label{sec:4-D_horizonless}
elements.sent-2411-131: We consider the case of a regular and asymptotically flat spacetime without any horizons.
elements.sent-2411-132: The local field formulation provides a clear method for analyzing the RSET in general spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-133: To find the vacuum state and corresponding RSET, all we have to do is to solve the field equations \eqref{eq:EOM_phi4d and \eqref{eq:EOM_psi4d and calculate the RSET~\eqref{eq:4DRSET_general .}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: By taking advantage of this method, we will demonstrate, through a case study, how the regularities of the solutions can indicate the proper vacuum state for the four-dimensional horizonless spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-135: It should be noted that if the background classical matter distribution contains discontinuities, such as in the case of thin shells, we need to establish junction conditions there.
elements.sent-2411-136: We will discuss the appropriate conditions in \appref{sec:JC .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Additionally, the auxiliary scalar fields and their RSET can have discontinuities in their derivatives if the background spacetime does, as in the case of well-known Hayward-type geometry \cite{Hayward:2005gi, Carballo-Rubio:2022nuj .}
elements.sent-2411-139: In the following, we demonstrate how the regularity of the auxiliary fields $\varphi$ and $\psi$, as well as the resulting RSET, lead to the preferred vacuum state using the Bardeen-type horizonless spacetime \cite{1968qtr..conf...87B, Carballo-Rubio:2022nuj as an illustrative example.}
elements.sent-2411-140: The Bardeen-type metric was originally proposed to describe a regular black hole without the central singularity \cite{1968qtr..conf...87B , and its metric is given as \begin{equation}\dd{s^2} =-f(r) \dd{t}^2+ f(r)^{-1} \dd{r}^2 +r^2 \dd{\Omega}^2, \label{eq:Bardeen_met}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}f(r)=1-\frac{2 m(r)}{r}, \quad m(r)=\frac{M r^3}{\qty(r^2+R^2)^{3/2}},\end{equation} with arbitrary parameters $M$ and $R$.}
elements.sent-2411-141: For sufficiently distant regions, $r \gg M \text{~and~ R$, the geometry reduces to the Schwarzschild spacetime with the ADM mass $M$.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Whether the spacetime has the event horizon is determined by a critical mass $M_\mathrm{crit / R = 3\sqrt{3 /4$, and is classified as follows, \begin{equation} M > M_crit \quad a regular BH with two horizons [5pt] M = M_crit \quad a regular BH with one degenerate horizon [5pt] M < M_crit \quad a horizonless regular spacetime \end{equation} Since we are now interested in the semiclassical effect on the horizonless regular spacetime, we focus on the case with $M < M_\mathrm{crit}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-198: In this work, we have investigated the possible vacuum state and the renormalized stress-energy tensors (RSETs) of massless quantum fields in two- and four-dimensional horizonless regular spacetimes with spherical symmetry and staticity.
elements.sent-2411-199: We employed the formalism established in \cite{Riegert:1984kt, Shapiro:1994ww, Balbinot:1999vg, Balbinot:1999ri, Mazur:2001aa, Mottola:2006ew that introduces local auxiliary scalar fields.}
elements.sent-2411-200: This formalism simplifies the evaluation of the RSETs into two steps: solving the field equations for the auxiliary fields and calculating those SETs as in classical field theory.
elements.sent-2411-201: While the inhomogeneous solutions of the scalar fields correspond to the local higher-curvature correction, the homogenous parts represent the non-local nature of quantum states and RSETs, i.e. the vacuum choice and the global properties of the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-202: Unlike formalisms with explicit regularization procedures, this method facilitates an extended discussion into more general spacetime cases.
elements.sent-2411-203: Furthermore, this formalism offers a systematic classification of possible vacuum states.
elements.sent-2411-204: This formulation is a useful one for understanding the effects of quantum fields on curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-205: In this framework, the boundary conditions determine the vacuum state associated with it.
elements.sent-2411-206: For a two-dimensional spacetime without horizons, the asymptotic Minkowski condition (vanishing scalar fields and RSET) at infinity and their regularities at the center are compatible, leading to the Boulware vacuum, which is similar to the Schwarzschild case.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, we found this is not the case for the four-dimensional spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-208: The asymptotic Minkowski condition at infinity and the central regularities are incompatible.
elements.sent-2411-209: Therefore, the Boulware vacuum is not realized, but instead, a non-trivial vacuum with a linearly increasing auxiliary scalar field is realized when we impose the central regularities as boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-210: This difference comes from the conformal non-flatness of the spherically symmetric spacetime that we have studied in four dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-211: The conformal flatness allows one to relate vacuum states in curved spacetime to those in flat spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-212: We have investigated the existence of the vacuum state associated with the Minkowski vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-213: In the two-dimensional case where the geometry is conformally flat, one can find the vacuum state that is associated with the Minkowski vacuum, with an additional inhomogeneous part.
elements.sent-2411-214: However, in the four-dimensional case where the conformal flatness is absent, such a vacuum state cannot be generally constructed.
elements.sent-2411-215: This results in the divergence of the auxiliary field either at the center or at infinity.
elements.sent-2411-216: The RSET of quantum fields on the compact star geometry and its backreaction on the geometry has been investigated in recent years \cite{Carballo-Rubio:2017tlh, Arrechea:2021xkp, Reyes:2023fde, Arrechea:2023oax , which implied the existence of ultracompact stars maintained by such semiclassical effects.}
elements.sent-2411-217: The previous works mainly assumed the Boulware vacuum for the fields, which is the most natural option for observers in the asymptotic flat region.
elements.sent-2411-218: However, the calculation of the stress-energy tensor shows divergent behaviors near the core of the star, requiring additional regularization such as the introduction of a cutoff radius.
elements.sent-2411-219: Our findings indicate that this divergence is likely due to the assumption of the Boulware vacuum, as it results in irregular behavior of the stress-energy tensor near the center.
elements.sent-2411-220: Instead, we propose prioritizing central regularity over sticking with the Boulware vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-221: As a result, the RSET we obtained remains regular throughout.
elements.sent-2411-222: This suggests that the physical vacuum for distant observers in asymptotically flat spacetime is not necessarily the Boulware vacuum, and must be determined in a way that is consistent with the global regularity of RSET.
elements.sent-2411-223: One may think that the discrepancy in the asymptotic behavior of the RSET at infinity~\eqref{eq:asymptotic_behavior_RSET seems similar to the result in \cite{Balbinot:1999ri, Balbinot:1999vg .}}
elements.sent-2411-224: In their analysis, the RSET obtained from the Reigert action \cite{Riegert:1984kt can reproduce the leading behavior near the horizon and at infinity for the Boulware state, the Unruh state, and the Israel-Hartle-Hawking state, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-225: On the other hand, they show that subleading behavior cannot be reproduced in each state.
elements.sent-2411-226: This comes from the fact that Weyl invariant terms are usually neglected when calculating the RSET from the anomaly-induced effective action.
elements.sent-2411-227: Such circumstances apply to our case as well.
elements.sent-2411-228: However, in our case, we are unable to reproduce even the leading behavior of the Boulware state since there is no degree of freedom to do so.
elements.sent-2411-229: Therefore, further investigation is needed to understand the origin of such behavior.
elements.sent-2411-230: In this work, we focused on the proper configuration of the static auxiliary scalar fields and their SETs in horizonless regular spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-231: Moving forward, it would be important to consider the evaluation of backreaction to the geometry (semiclassical analysis) for a more realistic approach as \cite{Carballo-Rubio:2017tlh, Arrechea:2021xkp, Reyes:2023fde, Arrechea:2023oax .}

elements.sent-2411-270: We examine the vacuum state and its corresponding renormalized stress-energy tensor (RSET) in static horizonless regular spacetime in both two and four dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-271: Furthermore, through a case study under the horizonless Bardeen-type spacetime, we demonstrate that the preferred vacuum state is not the Boulware vacuum, but a nontrivial one with a different RSET profile.
elements.sent-2411-272: Consequently, the renormalized stress-energy tensor (RSET) and its trace have a nontrivial expectation value.

elements.sent-2411-274: In the case of the four-dimensional Schwarzschild spacetime, there are three well-known vacuum choices: \begin{inparaenum [(I)] \item the Israel-Hartle-Hawking vacuum \cite{Israel:1976ur, Hartle:1976tp , which describes a BH in thermal equilibrium, \item the Unruh vacuum \cite{Unruh:1976db , which corresponds to an evaporating or collapsing BH with nonzero flux, \item the Boulware vacuum \cite{Boulware:1974dm, Boulware:1975fe , which coincides with the Minkowski vacuum as $r \rightarrow \infty$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-278: In particular, if low-frequency ($\ll 1/(radius )$) mode functions of quantum fields contribute non-negligibly to their RSET in the distant region, then the asymptotic behavior of the RSET with the Killing vacuum may exhibit nontrivial dependence on the internal structure of the horizonless compact object.

elements.sent-2411-335: Precisely speaking, what we have shown is that one of the following three conditions is incompatible with the others; \begin{inparaenum [(1)] \item regularities of the auxiliary scalar fields and RSET at $r=0$, \item Boulware state, \item the anomaly-induced effective action \cite{Riegert:1984kt or its local field formulation \cite{Riegert:1984kt, Shapiro:1994ww, Balbinot:1999vg, Balbinot:1999ri, Mazur:2001aa, Mottola:2006ew .}}}
elements.sent-2411-336: This work focused on the proper configuration of the static auxiliary scalar fields and their SETs in horizonless regular spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-337: In principle, the RSET could be computed by summing over mode functions of quantum fields, followed by regularization and subtraction of divergences.
elements.sent-2411-338: In regular spacetime, the mode functions should satisfy the regularity at the center.
elements.sent-2411-339: If the central regularity affects the long-distance behavior of low-frequency modes and if their contributions to the RSET is non-negligible, then we might observe nontrivial asymptotic behavior of the RSET in the distant region, similarly to how the (static and dynamical) tidal Love number of a regular compact object depends on the internal structure of the object.
elements.sent-2411-340: Such a cross-check analysis based on mode decomposition will deepen our understanding of semiclassical gravity.
elements.sent-2411-341: Particularly, a recent study by Mottola et al. \cite{Mottola:2023jlo focused on the two-dimensional case and suggested that various options of initial states, including nonvacuum ones, can be considered based on the local field formulation.}
